titleStmt.title: The Minister in Sweden ( (Johnson) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Minister in Sweden ( Johnson ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 2055. McClintockRobert M. McClintock, American Chargé in Finland. who has just arrived on courier trip wishes to report that last night Swedish Minister to HelsinkiHans Gustaf, Baron Beck-Friis. said that so far as his Government knew there was no truth in story reported in Helsinki’s 803, June 20, 11 a.m., of secret conversations between the Soviet and German MinistersAlexandra Kollontay and Hans Thomsen, respectively. here last April.The Chief of the Political Affairs Section of the Swedish Ministry for Foreign Affairs, S. J. Soderblom, who was reputed to know the members of the Soviet Legation most intimately, was reported in telegram No. 2199 from Stockholm, July 14, to be convinced that “there is no foundation for these reports.” (740.00119 European War 1939/1533) However, Baron Beck-Friis said that recently President RytiRisto Ryti, President of the Republic of Finland. had intimated to him his belief that such conversations had taken place. Beck-Friis suggested that possibly the Germans were propagating the story in Finland as part of their “war of nerves” to keep the Finns on the path of co-belligerency.For correspondence regarding the interest of the United States in Finland and in its possible withdrawal from war with the Soviet Union, see pp. 213 ff.

McClintock observed that if this were true the Germans were creating precisely an opposite effect as Ramsay’sC. Henrik Ramsay, Finnish Minister for Foreign Affairs. policy had somewhat altered since he began to think a Soviet-German peace more than a remote possibility.
Code text to Helsinki by courier.
